texts.orig: The continuing increase of energy consumption around the world, especially from fossil fuel sources, has resulted in releasing large amounts of CO2 to atmosphere. A recent report by the International Energy Agency (IEA) has revealed that 30 billion tons of CO2 was released in 2010, an increase of 10 billion ton from the 1990 value [1]. Several studies believe that the increase of the concentration of CO2, a major greenhouse gas, in the atmosphere is responsible for global warming and can cause dramatic effects on the world climate, such as more frequent occurrence of fl oods and storms, higher temperatures, and higher acidity of the ocean waters [2,3]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop ef fi cient technologies for capturing CO2 from its sources, especially from industries and power plants.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work presents an investigation of local gas side overall mass transfer coef fi cient ( KG ) of the membrane gas absorption process determined from CO2 concentration gradients. The experimental KG was compared with simulation results of non-wetted and partially wetted modes of operation from a plug fl ow and rated based model to study the effects of various operating parameters on the partial wetting of the membrane and its process performance. The experiments were performed using two types of membranes (polytetra fl uoroethylene, PTFE and polyvinylidene fl uoride, PVDF), and two absorbents (monoethanolamine, MEA and 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol, AMP). A comparison showed that the membrane wetting fraction was lowest at the liquid outlet and varied along the length of the membrane module due to the effect of pressure drop. The effect of liquid velocity on membrane wetting was very obvious at a low membrane wetting fraction whereas it was less noticeable at a higher membrane wetting fraction. The average membrane wetting fractions were lower when PTFE was used with MEA solution than when PVDF and AMP solutions were used in experiments. In addition, the results further showed that the use of CO2 loaded MEA solution could signi fi cantly reduce membrane wetting.

texts.orig: There are a variety of methods for CO2 capture, such as absorption into a liquid either water or amine solutions, adsorption on a solid, such as iron oxide, or impregnated activated carbon, cryogenic fractionation, or membrane systems. One of the most widely used and effective methods for CO2 separation is absorption with amine solutions. The major advantage of this method is that once the amines have been used to absorb CO2, they can be regenerated simply by heating. They are then ready to be reused in the CO2 absorption process. In terms of equipment, packed towers, bubble columns, and spray towers are commonly applied. However, these conventional equipments are not easy to operate because of many drawbacks and operational limitations, such as fl ooding, foaming, entraining, channeling, and high capital cost [4]. An alternative technology which can overcome these disadvantages and which possesses a high potential to replace conventional contactors for the CO2 capture is membrane contactor or the so-called membrane gas absorption systems. In this process, a hydrophobic membrane is employed as a phase barrier allowing two fl uids to contact with each other without the dispersion of one phase into the other. This makes membrane contactors easy to operate because the gas and liquid fl ow rates are independent, and as such, result in no fl ooding or foaming issues. The use of membranes,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, the membrane contactor also has its own disadvantages. Hydrophobic membranes can be partially wetted by organic solvents, resulting in low fl ux and short term usage of the membrane. The organic absorbents can penetrate into partial pores of the membrane because of their low surface tensions, and decrease mass transfer fl ux due to lower gas diffusivity in the wetted membrane phase. Moreover in a long-term operation, the organic absorbents can intrude into membrane matrix and signi fi cantly change the membrane morphology, pore size distribution, and membrane porosity [6], and will result in continuous deterioration of the mass transfer performance. To minimize the membrane wetting problem, the operating pressure should be kept below a critical value known as the penetration pressure. However, many researchers [7,8] have found that the partial wetting of the membrane can occur even if the pressure difference between the liquid and gas phases was kept lower than the penetration pressure, possibly due to capillary condensations of water and organic vapors in the membrane pores, and the nonuniform pore sizes of the membranes. The latter factor could lead to the penetration of liquid into the larger pores or membrane wetting could start from the largest to the smaller membrane pores.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since membrane wetting could signi fi cantly affect the performance of membrane gas absorption using amine solvents, several studies have attempted to estimate and evaluate the effects of operating parameters on the partial wetting of membranes. The Wilson plot method has been applied to determine the membrane mass transfer resistance that included the wetted membrane phase in both gas absorption and desorption using monoethanolamine (MEA) solvent [9,10]. However, a reaction occurring in the wetted membrane phase can cause an error in the estimations, and thus, this method cannot truly re fl ect the effect of operating parameters on the membrane wetting. An error in the estimations can occur when using this method to determine the membrane resistance in the case of the partially wetted mode with chemical reaction. The Wilson plot method requires a variation of the liquid velocity to obtain a value of the membrane mass transfer resistance. However, in real life situations, the change of liquid velocity also affects the mass transfer resistance of the wetted membrane phase as well as the degree of membrane wetting as reported in several works in the literature [11,12].Therefore this method cannot truly re fl ect the effect of operating parameters on the membrane wetting. Another method is to develop a model for estimating the mass transfer coef fi cient or absorption fl ux, and using membrane wetting (wetting fraction) as a fi tting parameter to match the simulation and experimental values. The theoretical value of the non-wetted mode when the gas totally fi lls the membrane pores is considered as the ideal performance of the process and the deviation between the experimental and simulation values is only due to the occurrence of partial wetting. This method was widely applied by many researchers. Mavroudi et al. [13] developed a mathematical model to predict the CO2 absorption performance in diethanolamine (DEA) solution using the cross fl ow membrane module and to estimate the membrane wetting in actual conditions. The study showed that the simulation results that assumed 1.5 -7% as the membrane wetting agreed well with the experimental data. Faiz et al. [7] compared the simulated CO2 fl uxes of different percentages of membrane wetting with the experimental results from literature sources. They found that the simulated fl uxes that considered 10% and 50% wetting were close to the experimental CO2 fl uxes of 0.005 mol/L MEA using PP membrane and 2 mol/L MEA using PVDF membrane, respectively. Boributh et al. [11] developed a model for a large scale membrane gas absorption for CO2 capture and proposed a membrane wetting correlation, which developed from the comparisons between experimental and simulation results, as the functions of liquid velocity and amine concentration.

texts.orig: In real operations, membrane wetting can be affected by many factors, including membrane properties itself (such as pore size, pore size distribution and membrane porosity)[14] and several operating parameters, namely inlet liquid pressure [15], liquid velocity, amine concentration [11], and gas and liquid contact time [16]. In addition, one of the parameters that could considerably affect membrane wetting is the change of liquid pressure along the length of the membrane fi ber due to pressure drop. Boributh et al. [17] and Goyal et al. [18] showed in their simulation studies that membrane wetting was not constant but varied with the change of liquid pressure along the length of the fi ber. These theoretical studies implied that the local overall mass transfer coeffceints were also not constant and could change due to membrane wetting. However, most of the works of membrane gas absorption in the literature reported only average mass trasnfer coef fi cients calculated from empirical approaches which considered only gas or liquid inlet and outlet conditions. They cannot re fl ect the effects of membrane wetting, pressure drop, and amine depletion on the change of mass trasnfer coef fi cent along the length of the membrane. Therefore, the objective of this study is to experimentally investigate the local overall mass transfer coef fi cent of the membrane gas absorption processes that could be affected by many factors, including the membrane wetting and also to study the effect of various operating parmeters on membrane wetting and mass tranfer performance of the process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, the local overall gas side mass tranfer coeffceints were determined from the CO2 concentration gradients in the gas phase. Three membrane modules connected in series with gas sampling points along the length of the module were used in the experiments. The membrane wetting was investigated by comparing the experimental results with the simulation data from a rate based model. The membrane wetting fraction or the extent of liquid fi lled in the membrane pores was estimated by using it as the fi tting parameter in the modelling to match the simulatated with the experimental absorption fl uxes. Various operating parameters that could possibly affect the membrane wetting were investigated, including the effect of membrane types (polytetra fl uoroethylene, PTFE, and polyvinylidene fl uoride, PVDF), amine solution types (MEA and 2-amino-2-methyl-1propanol, AMP), CO2 loading and liquid velocity.

texts.orig: There are two operating conditions often found in the membrane gas absorption processes, namely non-wetted and partially wetted modes as shown in Fig. 1. For the non-wetted mode, the mass transfer of gas into the liquid phase encounters three resistances in series, including the gas phase resistance for the diffusion of CO2 from the bulk gas to the outer surface of the membrane, membrane resistance for the diffusion within membrane pores and the liquid resistance for dissolution of CO2 into liquid solution. For the partially wetted mode, the resistance from the wetted membrane phase, where the liquid solution partially fi lled the membrane pores, is taken into consideration. The resistance-in-series models have been widely applied to calculate the overall mass transfer resistance and each individual resistance [19,20]. texts.orig: The reactions between CO2 and MEA and also CO2 and AMP can be described by the zwitterion mechanism [30]. The mechanism involves the formation of an intermediate zwitterion, which is subsequently deprotonated by a base to form a carbamate ion. In the case of MEA, the base in deprotonation step is mostly MEA itself; thus the overall reaction of CO2 and MEA could be as in Eq. (24) that one mole of CO2 reacts with two moles of MEA.

texts.orig: Along the length of the absorber, the membrane fi ber is divided into many small elements ( Δ Z) and the mass balance equations (Eqs. (15) -(17)) in each element are calculated with the local values of driving force (Eq. (9)) and overall mass transfer coef fi cient calculated by the resistance-in-series model (Eq. (1) or (2) for the nonwetted and partially wetted mode, respectively). The physical and chemical properties were called as a sub-function in the main program to calculate individual mass transfer coef fi cient. The detailed correlations for the physical properties of MEA solutions can be found in Rongwong et al. [27] while for the AMP solution can be found in the literature as reported in Table 2. The CO2 concentration in equilibrium with liquid bulk of MEA and AMP solutions was calculated from Eqs. (18) and (22), respectively. The F CO2 , X CO2 , and Ca min e for the next segment were calculated from Eqs. (15) -(17) and were computed along the length of the column until z equals to the membrane length (0.6 m). Then, the calculated X CO2 at the liquid inlet ( z ¼ 0.6 m) is compared with the experimental value. If the calculated and experimental values do not match, the new values of outlet X CO2 are guessed and the simulation repeated again from the top of the column ( z ¼ 0) until the difference between the calculated and experimental values are lower than 0.1%.

texts.orig: Figs. 5a and 6b show the CO2 concentration pro fi les in gas phase and the local overall gas side mass transfer coef fi cients ( KG ) of the membrane gas absorption using PTFE membrane, respectively. The gas fl ow rate and the liquid velocity were 3 L/min, and 0.036 m/s, respectively, and fresh 2 mol/L MEA solution was used as the absorbent. The y -axis represents the height of the membrane module in which the gas inlet and outlet were at Z ¼ 0 and 0.6 m, respectively. In the fi gures, experimental data were compared with simulation results of the non-wetted (0% wetting) and partially wetted (5% wetting) modes. Fig. 5a clearly shows that the experimental CO2 concentration in the gas phase continuously decreased along the length of the fi bers due to CO2 being absorbed and transferred to the liquid phase along the column. Comparisons of experimental with simulation results revealed that the experimental CO2 in the gas phase was closer to the simulation data of the 5% wetting than the non-wetted mode. Even though several studies reported that the membrane gas absorption using PTFE membrane can be operated in the non-wetted mode because of the high hydrophobicity of PTFE [27], the slight wetting of PTFE membrane in this study could possibly be due to the operating parameters and characteristics of membrane used. In this work, a longer membrane module (0.6 m) was used compared with those reported in the literature, thus a higher inlet liquid pressure was required for the amine solution to pass through the module, resulting in a higher pressure drop and membrane wetness [17]. In addition, the pore size of the membrane was also bigger. All of these could lower the penetration pressure which resulted in the partial wetting of the membrane pores.

texts.orig: The comparisons also show that the partial membrane wetting along the length of the column was not constant. The local experimental KG values were closer to the simulation results of the non-wetted mode at the fi rst module and the closest at the top of module. This is because this location was where the liquid pressure was lowest because the pressure drop inside the membrane fi ber and the gas pressure was the highest at the entrance. Therefore, the penetration of amine solution into the membrane pores was more dif fi cult in this region. However from this aspect, the membrane wetting should be the largest at the bottom of the third membrane module where the liquid pressure was the highest, but it was found that the local experimental KG of the third module seemed to be closer to the non-wetted mode than those of the second module. This might be due to the large excessive amount of MEA at the liquid inlet, for which some CO2 will react instantaneously such that the reaction plane becomes located only at the gas -liquid interphase. This decreased the importance of wetted membrane and liquid phase resistances, thus resulting in a higher experimental KG than those of the 5% wetting. Many researchers also reported that membrane wetting along the length of the fi ber was not constant and continuously changed from the effect of pressure drop of liquid fl ow in membrane fi ber [17,18]. However in this work, this phenomenon was obvious only in the fi rst and second modules.

texts.orig: The comparisons of experimental and simulated CO2 fl uxes of PVDF membrane are shown in Fig. 8. The gas fl ow rate, range of liquid velocities, and the absorbent used were the same as in the PTFE experiments. It was found that the experimental CO2 fl uxes were much lower than the simulation results of the non-wetted mode and close to the simulation results of 50% wetting. The degrees of membrane wetting of the PVDF membrane were much higher than those of the PTFE membrane because of the lower membrane hydrophobicity [9] and the higher membrane porosity [46] of the PVDF membrane as compared to those of the PTFE membrane. The higher membrane wetting also resulted in lower CO2 fl uxes for PVDF membrane as compared with those for PTFE membrane. The penetration pressures calculated from Eq. (8) for the PTFE and PVDF membranes using the contact angle (average value from Khaisri et al. [9]) and surface tension of water (Rongwong et al. [47]) were 2.7 and 0.9 bar, respectively. The signi fi cant difference of the penetration pressure between these two membranes mainly comes from the value of cos θ ( � 0.57 and � 0.1 for the PTFE and PVDF membranes, respectively). Therefore, even though the pore size of the PVDF membrane is smaller and the pore size distribution is narrower than those of the PTFE, the much lower membrane hydrophobicity of the PVDF membrane would result in the higher degrees of membrane wetting.

texts.orig: The wetting fractions of PTFE and PVDF membranes at different liquid velocities were estimated by adjusting the degree of membrane wetting in the modeling until the simulated fl ux matched with the experimental data. The results are shown in Fig. 9. For the PTFE membrane, the increase of the wetting fraction was not linear with the liquid velocity. The wetting fraction largely increased at the low liquid velocity but tends to be a constant at higher liquid velocities. This is because the membrane pore size reported in Table 3 is only the average pore size. There are pores with larger sizes than the average (as can be seen from Fig. 3) that can easily be wetted by the solution. However, the fraction of these bigger pores is limited, and thus, the wetting fraction tends to be constant at a certain number. This was also observed in the work of Boributh et al. [11]. For the PVDF membrane, the change of wetting fraction with liquid velocity was not signi fi cant. This is because the wetting fractions of PVDF membrane already reached its critical number where all the big pores were wetted, and thus wetting was no longer affected by liquid velocity.

texts.orig: The local overall gas side mass transfer coef fi cient of AMP solution using PTFE membrane was investigated and compared with the simulation results of the non-wetted mode and partially-wetted mode as shown in Fig. 12. It was found that the experimental KG was close to the simulation results considering 20% wetting, which was higher than the 5% wetting found in the case of MEA solution (Fig. 5b). It is reported that the surface tension of AMP solution was lower than that of the MEA solution [47]; therefore, it was easier for the AMP solution to seep into the pores of the membrane. It was noticed that the change of local KG along the column with liquid fl ow of AMP solution was lower than that of MEA solution. The reason is that only one mole of AMP is required to react with one mole of CO2 while two moles of MEA are required to react with one mole of CO2. Consequently, the change of local KG , which is mostly due to the change of free amine concentration available for the reaction with CO2 in the liquid phase of AMP solution, is less signi fi cant than that of the MEA solution. texts.orig: rate constant of AMP [32]. The effect of liquid velocity on the membrane wetting of PTFE membrane using AMP solution was not signi fi cant. The change of wetting fraction in the range of experiments conducted was found to be less than 3%. From the results of Figs. 8, 10 and 13, it was found that the effect of liquid velocity on the membrane wetting at the high membrane wetting fraction ( 4 20% wetting) was not noticeable. This is possibly due to the fact that membrane wetting in these experiments had already reached a condition where all the bigger pores than average were wetted due to the low surface tension of the absorbents. This resulted in a signi fi cant reduction of CO2 fl ux, and the wetted membrane phase thus becomes the major resistance for mass transfer. The slight change of the extent of liquid fi lled pore due to the change of liquid pressure affected the CO2 fl ux less in this region because it also increased the CO2 concentration at the gas -liquid interphase. The increase of liquid extent in the membrane pores lowered the CO2 absorption rate; thus the CO2 concentration in the gas phase as well as at the gas -liquid interface was higher than those in the case of non-wetted mode [50], resulting in less reduction of CO2 fl ux from the membrane wetting due to a higher mass transfer driving force of the wetted membrane phase.
